Even though Option C directly addresses incomplete removal of necrotic materials, unobturated accessory canals could serve as reservoirs where pathogens remain undisturbed and multiply over time, potentially exacerbating the persistence and enlargement of the periapical lesion. Thus, considering both incomplete debridement and overlooked anatomical complexities provides a more holistic understanding of recurrent infections.

Therefore, my revised answer aligns closely with option **C**, acknowledging additional support through consideration of unobturated accessory canals contributing to the problem. Final
